---
title: "01_simple-linear-regression"
output: html_document
date: "2026-06-01"
---

```{r}
#############
### SETUP ###
#############

# install.packages(c("ggplot2", "tidyverse"))
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)
```

```{r}
##############
### PART 1 ###
##############

# load data
mlb_team_seasons = read.csv("~/Desktop/01_mlb-team-seasons.csv")

# transformed regression data for estimating alpha
pythag_data = mlb_team_seasons %>%
  mutate(
    logit_wp = log(WP / (1 - WP)),
    log_rs_ra = log(RS / RA)
  )

# Task 1:
# no-intercept model: log(WP / (1 - WP)) = alpha * log(RS / RA)
alpha_model = lm(logit_wp ~ 0 + log_rs_ra, data = pythag_data)

summary(alpha_model)

# alpha estimate and uncertainty
alpha_hat = coef(alpha_model)[["log_rs_ra"]]
model_summary = summary(alpha_model)
alpha_se = model_summary$coefficients["log_rs_ra", "Std. Error"]
t_crit = qt(0.975, df = df.residual(alpha_model))

alpha_ci_lower = alpha_hat - t_crit * alpha_se
alpha_ci_upper = alpha_hat + t_crit * alpha_se
alpha_ci_95 = c(alpha_ci_lower, alpha_ci_upper)

alpha_summary = tibble(
  alpha_hat = alpha_hat,
  standard_error = alpha_se,
  ci_lower_95 = alpha_ci_lower,
  ci_upper_95 = alpha_ci_upper
)

alpha_summary

# helper function for converting exponent alpha back to predicted WP
pythag_wp = function(rs, ra, alpha) {
  rs^alpha / (rs^alpha + ra^alpha)
}

# Task 2:
# compare fitted-alpha model vs Bill James alpha = 2
pythag_data = pythag_data %>%
  mutate(
    wp_hat_alpha = pythag_wp(RS, RA, alpha_hat),
    wp_hat_bj = pythag_wp(RS, RA, 2),
    resid_alpha = WP - wp_hat_alpha,
    resid_bj = WP - wp_hat_bj
  )

# RMSE function
rmse = function(y, yhat) {
  sqrt(mean((y - yhat)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
}

rmse_alpha = rmse(pythag_data$WP, pythag_data$wp_hat_alpha)
rmse_bj = rmse(pythag_data$WP, pythag_data$wp_hat_bj)

rmse_summary = tibble(
  model = c("Estimated Alpha Model", "Bill James Alpha = 2"),
  rmse = c(rmse_alpha, rmse_bj)
)

rmse_summary

if (rmse_alpha < rmse_bj) {
  print("The estimated alpha model has a smaller RMSE.")
} else {
  print("Bill James's alpha = 2 model has a smaller RMSE.")
}

# Written response for Task 2: The model with the smaller RMSE has better predictive accuracy because its predictions are closer to the actual win percentages on average. In the actual vs. predicted plot below, points closer to the dashed 45-degree line represent more accurate predictions. If the estimated-alpha points are generally closer to the dashed line than the Bill James points, then the estimated-alpha model fits better. If the Bill James points are closer, then the alpha = 2 model fits better.

# create actual vs predicted plot for both models
plot_data = pythag_data %>%
  select(WP, wp_hat_alpha, wp_hat_bj) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(wp_hat_alpha, wp_hat_bj),
    names_to = "model",
    values_to = "predicted_wp"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    model = case_when(
      model == "wp_hat_alpha" ~ paste0("Estimated Alpha = ", round(alpha_hat, 3)),
      model == "wp_hat_bj" ~ "Bill James Alpha = 2"
    )
  )

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = predicted_wp, y = WP, color = model)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Actual vs. Predicted Win Percentage",
    subtitle = "Dashed line represents perfect prediction",
    x = "Predicted Win Percentage",
    y = "Actual Win Percentage",
    color = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Task 3:
# Report alpha_hat, standard error, and 95% confidence interval
alpha_hat
alpha_se
alpha_ci_lower
alpha_ci_upper

# Check whether alpha = 2 is plausible
alpha_2_plausible = 2 >= alpha_ci_lower & 2 <= alpha_ci_upper
alpha_2_plausible

# Written response for Task 3: The estimated Pythagorean exponent is alpha_hat. The standard error is alpha_se. The 95% confidence interval is from alpha_ci_lower to alpha_ci_upper. This interval gives a plausible range of values for the true Pythagorean exponent based on the data. If alpha_2_plausible is TRUE, then Bill James's alpha = 2 is plausible because 2 falls inside the 95% confidence interval. If alpha_2_plausible is FALSE, then Bill James's alpha = 2 is not plausible because 2 falls outside the 95% confidence interval.

# Task 4:
# residual diagnostic plot for fitted Pythagorean model
ggplot(pythag_data, aes(x = wp_hat_alpha, y = resid_alpha)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Residual Plot for Estimated Pythagorean Model",
    x = "Fitted Win Percentage",
    y = "Residual: Actual WP - Predicted WP"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Written response for Task 4: If the fitted Pythagorean model is reasonable, the residuals should be randomly scattered around 0. There should not be a clear curve or pattern in the residuals. The spread of the residuals should look roughly constant across fitted values. Curvature would suggest the model is missing some nonlinear structure. A fan shape would suggest changing spread. Large isolated points would suggest possible outliers. Use the residual plot above to comment on whether you see curvature, outliers, or changing spread.
```


```{r}
##############
### PART 2 ###
##############

# setup
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

# load packages
library(tidyverse)

# load data
mlb_payrolls = read_csv("~/Desktop/01_mlb-payrolls.csv")

# remove 2020 covid-shortened season and create log payroll variable
payroll_data = mlb_payrolls %>%
  filter(year_id != 2020) %>%
  mutate(
    log_payroll_median_ratio = log(payroll_median_ratio)
  )

# Model A: wp as a function of payroll median ratio
model_a = lm(wp ~ payroll_median_ratio, data = payroll_data)

# Model B: wp as a function of log payroll median ratio
model_b = lm(wp ~ log_payroll_median_ratio, data = payroll_data)

# fitted values and residuals for both models
payroll_data = payroll_data %>%
  mutate(
    fitted_a = predict(model_a, newdata = payroll_data),
    resid_a = wp - fitted_a,
    fitted_b = predict(model_b, newdata = payroll_data),
    resid_b = wp - fitted_b
  )

# RMSE function
rmse = function(y, yhat) {
  sqrt(mean((y - yhat)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
}

# compare model fits numerically
rmse_a = rmse(payroll_data$wp, payroll_data$fitted_a)
rmse_b = rmse(payroll_data$wp, payroll_data$fitted_b)

model_fit_summary = tibble(
  model = c("Model A: Payroll Median Ratio", 
            "Model B: Log Payroll Median Ratio"),
  rmse = c(rmse_a, rmse_b),
  r_squared = c(summary(model_a)$r.squared, summary(model_b)$r.squared)
)

model_fit_summary

# create prediction grid for regression lines
payroll_grid = tibble(
  payroll_median_ratio = seq(
    min(payroll_data$payroll_median_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),
    max(payroll_data$payroll_median_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),
    length.out = 200
  )
)

# add log variable to grid
payroll_grid$log_payroll_median_ratio = log(payroll_grid$payroll_median_ratio)

# predictions for both models
payroll_grid$pred_a = predict(model_a, newdata = payroll_grid)
payroll_grid$pred_b = predict(model_b, newdata = payroll_grid)

# model labels
model_a_name = paste0("Model A: Linear Payroll Ratio, RMSE = ", round(rmse_a, 3))
model_b_name = paste0("Model B: Log Payroll Ratio, RMSE = ", round(rmse_b, 3))

# model predictions plotting
payroll_grid_long = payroll_grid %>%
  select(payroll_median_ratio, pred_a, pred_b) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(pred_a, pred_b),
    names_to = "model",
    values_to = "predicted_wp"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    model_label = case_when(
      model == "pred_a" ~ model_a_name,
      model == "pred_b" ~ model_b_name
    )
  )

# highlight Oakland A's and New York Yankees
highlight_teams = payroll_data %>%
  mutate(
    team_label = case_when(
      team_id == "OAK" ~ "Oakland A's",
      team_id %in% c("NYA") ~ "New York Yankees",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(team_label))

# Task 1 plot: wp against payroll median ratio with both model fits
ggplot(payroll_data, aes(x = payroll_median_ratio, y = wp)) +
  geom_point(
    color = "gray70",
    alpha = 0.45,
    size = 1.5
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = payroll_grid_long,
    aes(
      x = payroll_median_ratio,
      y = predicted_wp,
      linetype = model_label
    ),
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = highlight_teams,
    aes(shape = team_label),
    size = 3,
    stroke = 1.2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Win Percentage vs. Payroll Median Ratio",
    subtitle = "Oakland A's and New York Yankees points are marked",
    x = "Payroll / Median Payroll",
    y = "Win Percentage",
    linetype = "Regression Model",
    shape = "Highlighted Team"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

# comparison of RMSE for both models
if (rmse_a < rmse_b) {
  better_model = "Model A has the smaller RMSE, so it fits better numerically."
} else {
  better_model = "Model B has the smaller RMSE, so it fits better numerically."
}

#The plot compares two models of the relationship between payroll and win percentage. Model A uses payroll_median_ratio directly, so it assumes each additional unit of payroll has the same effect on win percentage. Model B uses log_payroll_median_ratio, so it allows for diminishing returns to payroll. Visually, the better model is the one whose fitted line follows the cloud of points more closely.

# Task 2: residual plot for Model A
ggplot(payroll_data, aes(x = fitted_a, y = resid_a)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.55) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Residual Plot: Model A",
    subtitle = "wp predicted by payroll_median_ratio",
    x = "Fitted Win Percentage",
    y = "Residual: Actual WP - Fitted WP"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

# Task 2: residual plot for Model B
ggplot(payroll_data, aes(x = fitted_b, y = resid_b)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.55) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Residual Plot: Model B",
    subtitle = "wp predicted by log_payroll_median_ratio",
    x = "Fitted Win Percentage",
    y = "Residual: Actual WP - Fitted WP"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )


#Task 2 residual plot analysis: In a reasonable residual plot, the points should be randomly scattered around 0 with no obvious curve. Curvature would suggest that the model is missing nonlinear structure. A fan shape would suggest changing spread. Large isolated points may be outliers. The better diagnostic fit is the model with less curvature and more even residual spread.

# average residual by team for Model A, converted to wins
team_resid_a = payroll_data %>%
  group_by(team_id) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_resid_wins_a = mean(resid_a, na.rm = TRUE) * 162,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    over_under = if_else(
      avg_resid_wins_a >= 0,
      "Won more than predicted",
      "Won fewer than predicted"
    )
  )

# average residual by team for Model B, converted to wins
team_resid_b = payroll_data %>%
  group_by(team_id) %>%
  summarize(
    avg_resid_wins_b = mean(resid_b, na.rm = TRUE) * 162,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    over_under = if_else(
      avg_resid_wins_b >= 0,
      "Won more than predicted",
      "Won fewer than predicted"
    )
  )

# average residual graph for Model A
ggplot(
  team_resid_a,
  aes(
    x = fct_reorder(team_id, avg_resid_wins_a),
    y = avg_resid_wins_a,
    fill = over_under
  )
) +
  geom_col(width = 0.75) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Team Performance Relative to Payroll",
    subtitle = "Model A: Payroll Median Ratio",
    x = "Team",
    y = "Average Residual in Wins",
    fill = "Interpretation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8)
  )

# average residual graph for Model B
ggplot(
  team_resid_b,
  aes(
    x = fct_reorder(team_id, avg_resid_wins_b),
    y = avg_resid_wins_b,
    fill = over_under
  )
) +
  geom_col(width = 0.75) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Team Performance Relative to Payroll",
    subtitle = "Model B: Log Payroll Median Ratio",
    x = "Team",
    y = "Average Residual in Wins",
    fill = "Interpretation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8)
  )

# written response for average residual graphs: A positive average residual means the team won more games than predicted by the payroll model. A negative average residual means the team won fewer games than predicted by the payroll model. Because the residuals are multiplied by 162, the y-axis is interpreted as approximate wins above or below expectation. Teams near the top overperformed relative to payroll, while teams near the bottom underperformed relative to payroll.

# Task 3: choose one team-season
row_idx = 1
selected_row = payroll_data[row_idx, ]

# fitted win percentage using Model B
selected_fitted_wp = predict(model_b, newdata = selected_row)

# confidence interval for mean win percentage at that payroll level
ci_model_b = predict(
  model_b,
  newdata = selected_row,
  interval = "confidence",
  level = 0.95
)

# prediction interval for one individual team-season at that payroll level
pi_model_b = predict(
  model_b,
  newdata = selected_row,
  interval = "prediction",
  level = 0.95
)

# organize CI and PI results
interval_summary = tibble(
  interval_type = c("Confidence interval", "Prediction interval"),
  fitted_wp = c(ci_model_b[, "fit"], pi_model_b[, "fit"]),
  lower_bound = c(ci_model_b[, "lwr"], pi_model_b[, "lwr"]),
  upper_bound = c(ci_model_b[, "upr"], pi_model_b[, "upr"]),
  width = upper_bound - lower_bound
)

selected_row
interval_summary

round(selected_fitted_wp, 3)

# Task 3 written response: For the selected team-season, Model B predicts a win percentage of 0.469. The 95% confidence interval gives a plausible range for the average win percentage of teams with this same payroll level. The 95% prediction interval gives a plausible range for one individual team-season with this payroll level. The prediction interval is wider than the confidence interval because it accounts for both uncertainty in the estimated average relationship and the extra randomness of an individual team-season. In other words, it is easier to estimate the average outcome for teams with a given payroll than to predict the exact outcome for one specific team-season.

```
